Herod also put the tidal action of the Mediterranean Sea to good use. He constructed the city with a sewer system open to the sea and used the high tides to flush the sewer. This measure certainly cut down on the city's appeal as a seaside swimming resort but was an effective and labor-saving way to keep the city clean. "Profited" means he was above others in advancing knowledge as one of the Pharisees, which Paul calls "the most straitest sect of our religion." (Acts 26:5.) (George A. Horton, Jr, in Robert L. Millet, ed., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 6: Acts to Revelation, p.98)

Dictionary tells us that the Pharisees were separatists, who “prided themselves on their strict observance of the law, and on the care with which they avoided contact with things gentile.” Yet, ironically, the strictest Pharisee of them all was the greatest champion of the gentiles. Paul wrote of himself, ‘after the most straitest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.’ (Acts 26:5) Yet Paul understood much better than his old colleagues how Christ had fulfilled the Law of Moses. In principle,…
